1.pericope_label: All Nations Called to Praise YHWH
1.synthesis: Psalm 117 is the shortest psalm and the shortest chapter in the entire Bible, containing only two verses and thirty-three words in the Hebrew. Yet in those two verses the Psalter reaches its widest scope: “Praise the Lord, all nations! Extol him, all peoples! For great is his steadfast love toward us, and the faithfulness of the Lord endures forever. Praise the Lord!” (Ps 117:1–2). The paradox at the psalm’s heart is the word “toward us” (’aleinu): the reason all nations are called to praise YHWH is that his hesed has been great specifically toward Israel. The particularity of covenant faithfulness to one people generates the universal summons to praise. The psalm stands between the extended individual thanksgiving of Psalm 116 and the epic Hallel liturgy of Psalm 118, functioning as a hinge that broadens the scope from one person to all peoples before the great processional psalm opens.
1.historical_context: Psalm 117’s two verses are among the most significant in the New Testament, because Paul quotes them explicitly in Romans 15:11 as part of a catena of Old Testament texts (vv. 9–12) demonstrating that the Gentile mission was always part of YHWH’s plan: the inclusion of all nations in the praise of YHWH was not a Pauline innovation but the eschatological goal of Israel’s covenant relationship with YHWH. The “faithfulness of the Lord endures forever” (’emunah) grounds the universal praise in the permanence of YHWH’s character, connecting it to the hesed-and-’emet formula of Exodus 34:6. As the Hallel psalm sung at Passover, Psalm 117 was part of what Jesus sang at the Last Supper.
1.literary_structure: Psalm 117’s two verses follow the classic summons-grounds structure of the descriptive praise psalm. Verse 1 issues the summons: all nations, all peoples, praise and extol. Verse 2 provides the theological grounds (“for”): YHWH’s great hesed toward us and his enduring ’emunah (faithfulness). The “Praise the Lord” (Hallelujah) frame opens and closes with doxology. The brevity of the psalm is itself significant: the universality of YHWH’s claim requires no elaboration. The entire psalm is two claims: all nations should praise, because YHWH’s covenant faithfulness to Israel is the foundation of that universal praise.
1.theological_application: Psalm 117 refuses to dissolve the tension between YHWH’s particular covenant with Israel and his universal claim over all nations. The nations are called to praise YHWH not because he has a covenant with them identical to his covenant with Israel but because his faithfulness to Israel reveals the character of the one who created and governs all peoples. The particularity of election is therefore not a barrier to universal worship but its foundation: only a God who has demonstrated his character in concrete historical covenant faithfulness is worthy of universal trust and praise. Paul’s argument in Romans 9–11 develops precisely this logic.
1.christology: Paul places Psalm 117 at the center of his scriptural argument in Romans 15:7–13 for the mutual welcome of Jewish and Gentile believers. Christ is “a servant to the circumcised to show God’s truthfulness, in order to confirm the promises given to the patriarchs, and in order that the Gentiles might glorify God for his mercy” (Rom 15:8–9). The hesed that Psalm 117 celebrates toward Israel is validated and extended in Christ’s atoning work; his cross is the event that makes the universal summons of Psalm 117 not merely a pious aspiration but a reality now being enacted across every nation, tribe, and tongue (Rev 7:9).
